ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic scone entry (FOODON_03315091). Ham and Scallion Scones are a savory Chinese laminated pastry; the generic scone term is used as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor. No specific Shanghainese savory pastry entry exists in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pastry products) is the closest defensible category for this laminated savory pastry. No specific code for Shanghainese ham-and-scallion scones exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for this traditional Shanghainese savory pastry as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one scone piece weighing approximately 80 grams, providing around 272 kcal, 7.6 g protein, 14.8 g fat, and 27.6 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Scone, savory, with ham and green onion (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods closest match: biscuit/scone with meat filling)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Shanghainese ham-and-scallion scones exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for plain scones (FDC ID 18617), cooked ham (FDC ID 10179), and scallions (FDC ID 11291) in proportions consistent with the described recipe (approximately 60% dough/lard base, 25% ham, 15% scallion). Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against this composite estimate and found to be within plausible range; they have been retained with medium confidence. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 6.5 g/100g based on lard-dominant fat profile. Cholesterol estimated at approximately 35 mg/100g. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not available from approved sources for this composite preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Refined Wheat Flour Baked Products — analogous estimate based on plain scone / white-flour pastry data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Shanghainese ham-and-scallion scones. GI of 70 is estimated by analogy with plain scones and refined-wheat-flour pastries listed in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 65–75 for similar products). The fat and protein content from ham and lard moderately attenuate glycemic response, but the dominant refined-flour base sustains a high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 27.6) / 100 ≈ 19.3 g (high GL).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (520 mg/100g) from dry-cured Jinhua ham and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Ham and Scallion Scones; a single 80 g serving delivers approximately 416 mg sodium, representing about 18% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) and refined wheat flour base causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Ham and Scallion Scones only in small portions and infrequently, as the high GI and glycemic load of approximately 19 per serving can cause significant postprandial blood glucose spikes; pairing with a high-fibre food or protein source may partially attenuate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (approximately 6.5 g/100g from lard) and high sodium from cured ham
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Ham and Scallion Scones due to the combined burden of saturated fat from lard and high sodium from cured ham, both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and elevated blood pressure risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined flour and saturated fat may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While scallions provide antioxidant compounds and ham contributes protein and minerals, the high refined-carbohydrate and saturated-fat content of Ham and Scallion Scones may exacerbate inflammatory pathways; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume this food in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham and Scallion Scones (火腿葱油饼, Huǒtuǐ cōngyóubǐng), also known as Shanghai Savory Pastries, are a classic Shanghainese dim sum and breakfast item originating in Eastern China. Unlike the British sweet scone, these are savory, multi-layered, and flaky, resembling a laminated pastry similar to puff pastry in texture. The dough is made from refined wheat flour combined with lard or vegetable oil to create distinct flaky layers. The filling consists of finely chopped Jinhua ham — a prestigious Chinese dry-cured ham prized for its intense umami flavor — and fresh scallions that provide aromatic brightness. The preparation involves pan-frying to develop a golden-brown, crispy exterior, followed by high-heat oven finishing to ensure the interior layers are fully cooked and airy. Per 100 g, they provide approximately 340 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 18.5 g fat (of which approximately 6.5 g is saturated fat from lard), and 34.5 g carbohydrate with only 1.5 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is high at 520 mg/100g due to the cured ham. The glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high), making this food a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Clinically, portion control is advised for all three conditions. The food is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, or kosher diets due to pork content.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Shanghainese ham-and-scallion scones exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for plain scones, cooked ham, and scallions in recipe-consistent proportions. The AI-generated input values were cross-checked against this composite and found plausible; they have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar refined-wheat-flour pastries. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this traditional Shanghainese composite preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of April 2026. 2. saturated_fat_g (6.5 g/100g) and cholesterol_mg (35 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy lard and ham component data and should be treated as approximate. 3. The input dietary_tags incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'vegan'; these have been corrected to false as the product contains Jinhua ham (pork). 4. The input portions section contained a duplicate entry; only one default portion (80 g) is retained. 5. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for plain scones and refined-wheat-flour pastries (GI range 65–75); no direct measurement for this specific product was found. 6. Glycemic load per serving = (70 × 27.6) / 100 ≈ 19.3 g (high). 7. The input source (Shanghai Ocean University) is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central composite estimation as the approved evidence basis.
